annotator S.report_text:
The a!empts involved a phishing email appearing to be from the U.S. Depa"ment of State with links to zip #les containing malicious Windows sho"cuts that delivered Cobalt Strike Beacon. Phishing emails that contain links to malicious zipper files.

The a!acker appears to have compromised the email server of a hospital and the corporate website of a consulting company in order to use their infrastructure to send phishing emails. Adversaries may buy and/or steal capabilities that can be used during targeting.

The a!acker used unique links in each phishing email Phishing emails that contain links to malicious zipper files.

Cobalt Strike

The threat actor cra&ed the phishing emails to masquerade as a U.S. Depa"ment of State Public A%airs o$cial sharing an o$cial document. Adversaries may send spearphishing emails with a malicious link in an attempt to gain access to victim systems.

The links led to a ZIP archive that contained a weaponized Windows sho"cut #le hosted on a likely compromised legitimate domain, An adversary may rely upon a user opening a malicious file in order to gain execution.

The links led to a ZIP archive that contained a weaponized Windows sho"cut #le hosted on a likely compromised legitimate domain, Adversaries may attempt to manipulate features of their artifacts to make them appear legitimate or benign to users and/or security tools.

The sho"cut #le was cra&ed to execute a PowerShell command that read, decoded, and executed additional code from within the sho"cut #le. Adversaries may abuse PowerShell commands and scripts for execution.

The BEACON payload was con#gured with a modi#ed variation of the publicly available "Pandora" Malleable C2 Pro#le and used a command and control (C2) domain Adversaries may attempt to manipulate features of their artifacts to make them appear legitimate or benign to users and/or security tools.

The BEACON payload was con#gured with a modi#ed variation of the publicly available "Pandora" Malleable C2 Pro#le and used a command and control (C2) domain Commands to the remote system, and often the results of those commands, will be embedded within the protocol traffic between the client and server.

Each phishing email contained a unique malicious URL, likely for tracking victim clicks. Adversaries may send spearphishing emails with a malicious link.

ds7002.lnk was a malicious sho"cut (LNK) #le that contained an embedded BEACON DLL and decoy PDF, and was cra&ed to launch a PowerShell command. An adversary may rely upon a user opening a malicious file in order to gain execution.

ds7002.lnk was a malicious sho"cut (LNK) #le that contained an embedded BEACON DLL and decoy PDF, and was cra&ed to launch a PowerShell command. Adversaries may abuse PowerShell commands and scripts for execution.

ds7002.lnk was a malicious sho"cut (LNK) #le that contained an embedded BEACON DLL and decoy PDF, and was cra&ed to launch a PowerShell command. Adversaries may inject dynamic-link libraries (DLLs).

ds7002.lnk was a malicious sho"cut (LNK) #le that contained an embedded BEACON DLL and decoy PDF, and was cra&ed to launch a PowerShell command. Adversaries may transfer tools or other files from an external system into a compromised environment.

the PowerShell command extracted and executed the Cobalt Strike BEACON backdoor and decoy PDF. Adversaries may communicate using OSI application layer protocols.

This command included some speci#c obfuscation, which may indicate a!empts to bypass speci#c detection logic. For example, the use of 'FromBase'+0x40+'String', in place of FromBase64String, the PowerShell command used to decode base64. Adversaries may attempt to make an executable or file difficult to discover or analyze by encrypting, encoding, or otherwise obfuscating its contents on the system or in transit.

The PowerShell loader code is obfuscated Adversaries may attempt to make an executable or file difficult to discover or analyze by encrypting, encoding, or otherwise obfuscating its contents on the system or in transit.

The dropped BEACON loader DLL was executed by RunDll32.exe using the expo" function "PointFunctionCall": Adversaries may bypass process and/or signature-based defenses by proxying execution of malicious content with signed, or otherwise trusted, binaries.

annotator L.report_text:
The a!empts involved a phishing email appearing to be from the U.S. Depa"ment of State with links to zip #les Phishing emails that contain links to malicious zipper files.

containing malicious Windows sho"cuts that delivered Cobalt Strike Beacon. Malicious zipper files disguised as legitimate Windows shortcuts.

The a!acker appears to have compromised the email server of a hospital and the corporate website of a consulting company The attacker appears to have compromised the email server of a hospital and the corporate website of a consulting company in order to use their infrastructure to send phishing emails.

The a!acker used unique links in each phishing email Phishing emails that contain links to malicious zipper files.

the links that FireEye observed were used to download a ZIP archive that contained a weaponized Windows sho"cut #le, launching both a benign decoy document and a Cobalt Strike Beacon backdoor, customized by the a!acker to blend in with legitimate network tra%c. Malicious zipper files disguised as legitimate Windows shortcuts.

The threat actor cra&ed the phishing emails to masquerade as a U.S. Depa"ment of State Public A$airs o%cial sharing an o%cial document Phishing emails that contain links to malicious zipper files.

The links led to a ZIP archive that contained a weaponized Windows sho"cut #le hosted on a likely compromised legitimate domain, jmj[.].com. Malicious zipper files disguised as legitimate Windows shortcuts.

execute a PowerShell command that read, decoded, and executed additional code from within the sho"cut #le. The use of a malicious shortcut file to execute the code.

execute a PowerShell command that read, decoded, and executed additional code from within the sho"cut #le. The use of a PowerShell command to execute additional code.

the sho"cut #le dropped a benign, publicly available, U.S. Depa"ment of State form and Cobalt Strike Beacon The malicious shortcut file drops a seemingly innocuous U.S. State Department form to confuse and distract the user.

The BEACON payload was con#gured with a modi#ed variation of the publicly available "Pandora" Malleable C2 Pro#le and used a command and control (C2) domain – pandorasong[.]com – assessed to be a masquerade of the Pandora music streaming service. Use of a C2 domain that mimics a legitimate service, in this case Pandora music streaming service.

Each phishing email contained a unique malicious URL Sending phishing emails containing malicious URLs.

two variants

ds7002.lnk was a malicious sho"cut (LNK) #le that contained an embedded BEACON DLL and decoy PD The malicious shortcut file (LNK) is disguised as a legitimate file

to launch a PowerShell command Using a PowerShell command launched from the shortcut file (LNK).

On execution The malicious shortcut file (LNK) is executed by the user.

the PowerShell command extracted and executed the Cobalt Strike BEACON backdoor and decoy PDF The malicious shortcut file (LNK) is disguised as a legitimate file

This command included some speci#c obfuscation, which may indicate a!empts to bypass speci#c detection logic. This technique includes the use of obfuscation techniques to hide the malicious behavior of the malware. In the context described, the command included a specific type of obfuscation that may have been used to evade detection.

The PowerShell loader code is obfuscated This technique is applied when executable files or data are made less readable to avoid detection by security tools
